author: @billdaggett7911
text:
As an electrical engineer, I call BS on this one. That fan motor is 1/8 HP at most. It could never generate more than it's rated power without burning up. 1/8 HP = 746/8 = 93 watts if you could spin it fast enough. With the extended blades, no way. Realistically, this rig may be good for 10-15 watts of power. You would also need a circuit to condition the DC output to charge a battery. That's way over the head of anyone involved in this video. The 20v output is open circuit. Connected to a load, it's going to drop significantly. Volts aren't power. volts x amps = power. As soon as you put a load on that "generator" the blades will slow down since they have resistance against them. That will drop the voltage as well.

I could go on. This guy is getting lots of clicks. That's his goal. Making usable power is not.

author: @kenwebster5053
text:
I notice that you got some pitch twist into the blades.  It looks like you achieved that simply by tapering the LE but leaving the TE straight along the tube.  You could cut both edges slightly helically to achieve a calculated true pitch, so the whole blade is contributing it's share to torque loading the rotation.  This would get the generator up to speed in the lightest possible breeze, maximising it's efficiency in minimal conditions.  Basic aerodynamics such as L = 1/2 . p . V^2 . S . Cl etc would be sufficient to design the blade pitch & size.  Failing aerodynamics software, it could probably be designed in excel using Simpsons rule to sum the forces along the rotating blade.

The problem though is handling high wind.  Even a well balanced blade will experience loads (centrifugal & blade twist) in gale force winds.  Would you go with a tilt feathering  design as variable pitch seems a bit beyond the scope of a simple solution?  Would tilt feathering be sufficient, would you also need some over voltage protection, centrifugal brake etc?

text: Okay I can see how that kinda works.  It will need to be mounted on a sturdy pole as you said but it will also need some sort of vane that swivels it into the wind.  Also you might need some sort of brake to control the revs or it might self destruct in high winds.  My main question is where do you store the power you have generated and how do you use the power you have generated.  Obviously the answer is batteries but then you would need ac/dc convertor etc and this cheap project begins to get more technical.   I like these sort of ideas though....too often that little fan motor will be consigned to the skip after the Micra has died and then ends up in landfill or where-ever.  If people like you keep plugging away at this sort of stuff then one day something really simple and effective will arise. ..bit like Trevor Bayliss and his wind up radio and wind up torch....in effect so simple and everybody said why didn't I think of that.  Keep it up👍

author: @rowanflynn462
text: I hate to burst your bubble but no load voltage, which is what you measured is no indication of power. To illustrate this point, eight AAA dry cells wired in series will give you twelve volts. The same voltage as your car battery, but try using them to start your car. They cannot deliver the current, (amps) required. If your generator could maintain twenty volts while delivering even one amp of current I would be very impressed, not to mention surprised.

author: @matthewkleinmann
text: Two hints for drafting the lines out for cutting the pipe up.  Got these from years of making high powered rockets.  Wrap a strip of paper a few inches wide around the perimeter of the pipe, the exact width is not important.  Wrap it around once tightly and cut it so it just overlaps and tape it, so you have a band around the pipe.  Hold the pipe up in a door jamb and trace a straight line across the paper and cut it.  Fold the paper exactly in half and crease it.  Do it a second time.  Wrap the paper back around the pipe and tape it.  Now line one of the creases up in a door jamb and draft out that line.  Do the same with the other three.  This is pretty common for spacing rocket fins.  How much power were you able to get out of it?  I am guessing under 50W.  Not bad if you can get the fan for free.

replies.author: @ghz24
replies.text:
@trueaussie9230 Motors are rated in rpm per volt. What ever rpm it spins at when powered at a voltage, it must be spun that fast to generate that same voltage. 
For a radiator fan that's usually 1200 to 1500 rpm
It will require exceptionally high wind speeds to generate useable electricity.
Car radiator fans aren't designed to minimize cogging so I doubt if the generator starts spinning much below 15 mph wind speed. 
Virtually all real windmills have odd numbered blades for a reason.
The magnets in a car fan are usually ferrite magnets not neodymium
You can think what you want but for a small home wind generator you want a low rpm and or high voltage motor. 
Not saying it can't generate any electricity just it's a very poor choice due to its performance at low wind speed.

replies.author: @londonnight937
replies.text: @trueaussie9230 If you go and read some theory on electronics, voltage isnt power. You can have voltage on something, doesnt mean that that thing can produce much power. Power is when you have a load, or resistor connected to the generator, and a voltmeter in parallel to the resistor, and an ammeter in series with the resistor. When you can read both meters at the same time, and multiply the values, that's when you know what power your system can provide. You also have to do maximum power point tracking, which then would show you what max power your device could generate at a particular wind speed. That would be the important metric, not the voltage alone, nor amps alone.

author: @rogerphelps9939
text: Please do not bother with this. Although he got the thing to produce 20 volts he did not put a load on and measure the power generated. That would tell a completely different story. The power output of a wind generator is proportional to the cube of the wind speed times the square of the blade length. That is why proper wind turbines are huge things in very windy places.  Most of the time in most domestic locations this thing will be sitting idle. In very windy conditions you might get 100 watts out of it if you are lucky.  You are much better off using solar PV panels.

author: @eknott1000
text:
Great video! I worked with small wind generators for a number of years. I noticed a stress point, on the way the blades are mounted to the plastic fan blades, over time the original blades will stress crack and fail. If you ad a blade brace to tie in all four blades on the front side of the blades, you will eliminate much of the stress on the original fan and give your windmill more longevity!
Ed

author: @wafikiri_
text:
There is a reason why the original blades had a twist. So should the new blades. The blades' chord at the tips, if the blades are lengthy, should be almost perpendicular to the shaft's axis, whereas if the blades could reach the said shaft, the chord there would almost align with it (the "almost" words would vanish if aerodynamics did not require a certain angle of attack). So, at the blades' roots, the chord should have a noticeable angle between alignment with the shaft and perpendicular to it.
Nevertheless, most of the aerodynamic power is transfered to the blades at their outermost portion, the formula for that power includes the chords' radii cubed, reason why the innermost portion is not much important (but the twist helps prevent turbulent air flux, which much detriments efficiency).

author: @stevewindisch7400
text: Not all types of AC motors will work "running backwards" (operating as an alternator/generator). What is commonly used, is a permanent magnet device usually either a perm. mag. alternator (they are an after-market high-current upgrade for cars and boats), or a DC series-wound motor like you would find in an exercise treadmill. These will work even at slow revolutions, while motors without permanent magnets will not. Regarding looking at the output; you really need to have a load on it, perhaps some form of light bulb (incandescent not LED). This changes the voltage level to be lower and more realistic. But at any rate, the power needs to be run through a bridge rectifier and used as DC (... usually for battery charging), unless it was already DC from a DC motor run backwards or an alternator with a built-in rectifier. So if you expect to use this as a backup when the wall power is out... You still need a battery bank. It CANNOT be used as AC as it will not be the right voltage or frequency (which must be 50 or 60 Hertz).

author: @andersgrassman6583
text:
Nice idea. Now, my concern is, that probably the cheapest way to store the power, is using car batteries. But I put four of them in series, when I was 14, to make an arch welding setup. It operated intermittently successfully for perhaps a minute. However, during the high current discharge, the batteries had created very large quantities of hydrogen gas, which I didn't think of. Also, I had severely underestimated how thick the wiring needed to be. (I musn't ever have seen car jumper cables?!) The wires melted and somehow, some small spark was created, igniting the hydrogen gas outside and inside the the car batteries, causing two of of them to violently explode and hurling shattered batteryparts through the air, and presumably also acid. Perhaps luckily, I had my back turned at the explosion.

In conclusion, I since then carry great respect and awareness of the dangers associated with car batteries. Indeed any high capacity battery. And I feel hesitant to build a huge powerbank with them. It would have to sit in another shack / building away from my house. And then I would still feel I lived close to a minor bomb.,

author: @skeetersaurus6249
text: Add a cross-beam, directional vane, a DC Voltage Regulator and a charge regulator, and you're ready to connect it to a battery (to charge it). Also, if you notice the blades on any commercial fan, they are tapered large-end-out...that's because of force generated 'at distance' from the axis is further multiplied this way. Turn your blades around (tapered end towards motor) and you'll get greater RPM (and thus, higher voltage/amperage)...but the blades will need to be balanced, if you do this ('fulcrum' balancing should work for lower RPM ranges).

author: @Jiggledance
text: Make like 10 of them using a Rose blade, various sizes down to a Rose Bud, and mount it on a windward wall. Paint them the color of flowers with different color patterns or different pinwheel patterns and colors.  If it is too windy sometimes, add shrouds mounted separately that can automatically pop up in high winds and redirect some of the wind.  Shape and paint the pop-up shrouds like giant leaves.   The blades will still turn but shrouds will protect it from blowing apart.   Just use a simple lever that has tension to hold shrouds down at low winds and high winds pry them up.  Once wind goes down the shrouds go back in place. You can even design the shrouds to look like pedals that close and cover up the blade assembly.

replies.author: @buddhistsympathizer1136
replies.text:
Amperage? You mean the current? (In the UK).
What he really needs to do is to attach a low resistance load across the output and measure the potential (volts) across it and/or the current (amps) going through it.
Knowing both current through and the potential across the load (R=V / I) you can then calculate the power (in Watts) being generated (P=V x I).

replies.text: Exactly. Any nominal 12 V solar panel can create +20V open circuit too. The power it's able to generate varies with size of course. Put a load on it and watch the voltage drop. If the load is matched to the power rating of the source the voltage should stay stable at around 13-14 V or so. For example, a solar charge controller ensures that it only puts as much current into the batteries as it can while keeping the PV array voltage at around 14 V, enough to keep current flowing in the right direction.

author: @thereare4lights137
text:
How is the longevity on this?  Two key concerns would be the weak points on the fan hub where they attach.  The added mass of the longer blades might cause one to snap where it joins to the hub.  
Second would be the bushings or bearings.  Most of those electric cooling fans use bronze bushings which under the added mass of the fan blades, would probably wear rather quickly.  Roller bearings would probably mitigate against that. 
Still a great little project though that can teach people how easy it is to generate their own power, and to get involved with the scientific process.  👍🏻

text: Good project but the output voltage doesn't really tell how much power this device can produce. You need to attach an appropriate load and measure the wattage. author: @richardlanchester249
text:
I saw some interesting demonstrations of do-it yourself wind turbines made from recycled materials following a design from Hugh Piggott, aiming at a higher but not highest efficiency, with aerofoil section wood blades carved in a simple way that didn't require extreme carpentry skills, a car wheel hub assembly, and homemade generator parts made from coils of reused copper wire and little rare earth magnets from computer hard disc mechanisms set in moulded resin discs. 
This was by a group of engineers called V3, who did hands on DIY workshops at events like green festivals and Climate Camp demos.
Another thing to check out, experimenters in New Zealand have been using very efficient, flat direct-drive low speed brushless motors from a local washing machine company called Fischer & Paykel, to make various experimental generators, and I understand Samsung, LG, and Maytag USA and others are now making the same motor design under license.
And another thought, what are the possibilities of flat brushless DC motors (some with nice rare earth magnets) in electric-bike wheels? As these bikes get damaged and thrown away, it's now possible to find the wheels at rubbish tips. A combination of a steel disc brake on the hub, and a stiff wheel rim further out, should assist making a solid robust attachment for turbine blades.

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: @johnq8792 12 volts and 30 amps is possible depending on the motor you use... a "permanent magnet" motor generates electricity when you turn it the opposite direction... the amperage is determined by the size of the magnet and the number of coils... and the diameter of the wire windings

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: And the length and shape of the air foils... and the energy required to turn the motor fast enough to create enough energy to get past the diodes in the dioxide bridge that controls the flow of energy... that's why he was measuring 0.5 amps... most of the energy created goes away through heat at the diodes and friction in the bearings... it's possible to reduce all of the detrimental properties... but it is pretty heavy duty chemistry and metallurgy... and just a bit of maxwell's equations to measure the changes you made... it's not magic... it's physics

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text:
So... the length of the airfoil is like a lever... Archemedes said he could move the World with a long enough lever.... it's true 👍

The flight of our aircraft is a tremendous lever... and it, uses the passage of air to obtain lift... on levers... through the attraction to the Planet called gravity... it's moving the Planet an infinitesimal length... it's a pretty good trick...

text: I have had this kind of thing in my head for a while. Great to se that the principle works. Are the bearings in the existing motor up to carrying  the extra weight of the blades?

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: No

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: It's a good idea to use good bearings... most inexpensive motors use bushings... it's the expensive ones that have true bearings... however.. if you have a drill press... go find a nice flat river rock..  it's AuPt in one form or another... and 10 on the Moh's scale of hardness... drill the center hole to a very slightly smaller than needed hole... switch hole saws and.. you have a high speed materials that has a zero heat expansion coefficient... and will never wear out... the shaft will.. but not the bushings

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: AuPt has double double covalent bonds rather than metallic bonds... it doesn't pass current..  it's 10 on the Moh's scale of hardness... like diamond 💎

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: AuPt requires diamond bits to machine the stuff... it's not half and half... it's one thing... and they share double double covalent bonds... it's 10 000 times harder than ruby

replies.author: @richardcarew4708
replies.text: A specific gravity test reveals that it's either AuPt or silicon carbide... or carbon... carbon and silicon carbide are very light... feel warm to the touch... AuPt often comes in aluminum oxide in the heavy red river rocks.. it's absolutely ubiquitous... even in Merry old England

author: @RobMacKendrick
text:
Years ago I was teaching in a remote school on a small island in the North Pacific. One of my students built a wind turbine much like this one, except he used a car alternator to generate the power and whittled 4-foot blades out of 2x4s. He mounted it on a 20-foot pole outside the school shop, and when word got round (tiny school) that he was ready to raise it we all turned out to watch. 

The best description I can give is "terrifying". That 8' span instantly took the sea wind and the thing just started wamp-wamp-wamping like it would eat the world. Chris finally managed to stop the blades by turning them out of the wind (which meant he had to get up on a ladder right behind those freakin' whirling broadswords and push on the vane), but the concept was well-proven. I don't remember how much electricity he banked in the battery during that maiden run, but all who witnessed it undoubtedly remain impressed by the sheer power he'd harnessed.

author: @pietervanniekerk4453
text:
A very good idea to use this motor ... The motor - generator now should deliver more or less the same current it draws in the motor mode , however because it is a permanent magnet generator the average output voltage will be proportional to the speed it rotates at ..... those motors are designed to run at high speed .... you not going to get the RPM required with the long blades.... plenty torque ... and current but the voltage output will be low .
If you built a vertical axis generator using a bicycle wheel, a belt running on the rim could be used to drive this type of motor ( generator ), with a smaller pulley on the motor. the speed would be increased , secondly no pivot is required to point the blade into the wind. two or more of this type of motors can be driven by the same belt , connecting the motors in series will increase the output voltage. Lastly because the vertical blade system can not over rev and destroy itself. long blades are noisy and require higher winds to be effective.

author: @clivekennedy6616
text: The hardest bit is getting a decent commutator on the swivel for the mast mounting which  you haven't done. Also a car fan probably won't generate any usable power because it's designed to run from about 1000rpm which is too fast for wind where lower rev generation is crucial. You are getting it to spin out of control unloaded which is irrelevant, as soon as it's loaded it will pretty much stop due to resistance. Very poor effort.

author: @mpccenturion
text: Some 30 yrs ago, I was taking Mech Eng. Fluid Dynamics class peeked my interest and I had been looking at how to increase mileage in Gasoline engines. Well - Canada here - I had a full-size truck with a 225 Cu In engine. The fan - pulling air thru the radiator had to consume some portion of HP. So I pulled the mechanical fan and replaced it with a pair of fans from diesel Golf engines. I put two of them in and set one with a fan switch and the second was in line with a diesel Rad switch. When it got up to tea time - the fan would kick on.  Over 10,000 miles - I raised it to almost 29 mpg - Imperial gal - not the US wimp.  --- In the end - I know it added HP as I could make the clutch slip - running up hills. It never had the hp to do that before the work.  I figure it was worth 20 hp at least.   For reference - go look at how much  hp is soaked up by Super chargers, High amp alternators and the like. Cheers

author: @williamfraser
text: Very nice project. I am sure some have figured out by now that the trailing edge is in fact the leading edge. This means the fasteners are behind the axis where the wind force acts upon. The implication is that torsional blade flex during a gust will increase the blade's angle to the wind, further increasing the load and the possibility of damage. The slight sweep of what is in fact the leading edge mitigates the problem. The sweep also resulted in the correct direction of twist.

replies.author: @denislostinlondon199
replies.text:
Power = current x volts.
Power = volts x volts ÷ resistance.
Put a resistive load on the generator and measure the voltage across the resistor. Your calculation will give you the power.

replies.author: @denislostinlondon199
replies.text:
Then use Ohm's Law.
If Volts = Amps x Ohms,
Amps = Volts ÷ Ohms.
You have your current.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thank you, Robert.

replies.text: Use a diode in series between the battery and the wind generator. That way, when the wind does die down, the generator doesn't become a big fan, and back-drain the battery!

replies.author: @melindaeaton5717
replies.text: @vincentrobinette1507  could this fan be a curling fan operated without a battery straight through a fuse if the solar panel was a 100 watt 5 amp 12 v that puts out up to 19 volts? Just wanting to run fan during day.

replies.author: @vincentrobinette1507
replies.text: @melindaeaton5717 That actually works quite well.(forget the diode) I use a similar 36 cell solar panel, and an electric radiator fan out of a car as an attic ventilator fan. The sunnier the day, the harder the fan blows, which is needed, because the attic gets hotter. With less sun, the fan runs slower. The torque necessary to turn a fan goes up on the square of speed, but the power goes up on the cube. that makes a fan a near perfect load for a solar panel, without the need for any kind of power point tracking. It's just a matter of getting the blade pitch right, so the motor impedance is a good match for your particular panel.

author: @TheCreativeChemist
text: Dear Robert, I made iron chloride catalyzed hemp pyrolysis carbon. The carbon has great conductivity 7 ohm per cm. But my ink has a bad square conductivity of 1 to 10 kohm and none of my supercapacitors can hold on to their charge. I use 1 mm aluminium as current collector, pva glue as binder, 1M NaHSO4 as electrolyte and printer paper as separator. Could you help and tell me why the square conductivity is bad and the caps don't store their charge?

replies.author: @Jigaboo123456
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You mention the voltage it produces, but NOT the wattage, or even the rated wattage of the DC motor, which would be the max output achievable IF the device worked perfectly. I'm sure that you know that wattage (volts x amps )is power, not just volts. 
Before wasting time and money making one of these, myself and no doubt many others would really like to know what the practical power we could expect, and if you had just measured the amps after you'd done the volts (or given the motor's rating). 
Could you post this please, I'd like one for my off-grid weekend/holiday shack, but if it was only a few watts, it wouldn't be worth the bother.
Otherwise, good video, thanks for posting.
